coredata.dc:title: Application of surface nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in the sensing of ferric ions and glutathione: Spectroscopic investigations and DFT calculations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Zamir G.
Agrawal, Tanisha N.
Bari, Sanjaykumar B.
Nangare, Sopan N.
Patil, Pravin O.
coredata.dc:description: Developing a sensing platform that can quickly and accurately measure glutathione (GSH) is crucial for the early detection of various human diseases. GQDs have shown great potential in many technological and biological applications. This study focused on synthesizing nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) with stable blue fluorescence using a simple and easy hydrothermal method in one step. The bamboo fiber was used as the green source for this synthesis. The NGQDs had a tiny particle size of 4.7 nm and emitted light at 405 nm when excited. They displayed a remarkable quantum yield of 40.36 % and were effectively used as fluorescent probe to specifically detect Fe3+. The energy transfer mechanism led to the NGQDs' fluorescence being deactivated by Fe3+ ions (turn- “off”). However, with the addition of GSH to the system, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs was reactivated (turn- “on”). Thus, a fluorescence turn “off–on” system was developed for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and GSH. Using density functional theory (DFT), it was theoretically calculated that the surface of the fabricated NGQDs possess lone pairs of electrons on oxygens and doped nitrogen causing a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process to occur. This PET process was suppressed previously owing to complex formation between oxygen atoms of modeled structure and ferric ions. The sensing platform displayed a sensitive response to Fe3+ in the 1–1000 μM range with LOD of 34 nM and GSH in the range of 1–50 μM, with a detection limit of 45 nM. Furthermore, the NGQDs exhibited high selectivity towards Fe3+ and GSH over other electrolytes and biomolecules. Additionally, the probe exhibited non-cytotoxicity and was practically applicable for the detection of GSH in HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Glutathione
Green synthesis
Fluorescence biosensing
Bamboo fibre

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing study using NGQDs/Fe3+ for GSH
Sensing of serum sample for GSH
Confocal microscopy of probe for GSH detection
Analysis of GSH in human serum sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kavayitri Bahinabai Chaudhari North Maharashtra University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are highly obliged to the H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, (India), for providing necessary facilities. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by The Kavayitri Bahinabai Chaudhari North Maharashtra University, Jalgaon under Vice Chancellor Research Motivation Scheme (VCRMS), (KBCNMU/11A/VCRMS/Budget-2021-22/Sci. & Tech./137/2022.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The UV–vis spectrum of NGQDs; (b) PL spectra of NGQDs at different excitation wavelengths in range from 270 nm to 400 nm.
(a) The FTIR spectra of NGQDs; (b) EDX analysis of NGQDs; (c) Raman spectra of NGQDs showing G and D bands.
(a) The particle size analysis; (b) SEM image of NGQDs; (c) TEM images NGQDs inset showing single NGQD particle; (d) SEAD pattern of NGQDs.

(a) The PL spectra of NGQDs in presence of Fe3+ (1–1000 μM); (b) trend of change in fluorescence intensity with gradual addition of Fe3+ with inset showing the fluorescence of NGQDs in (a) absence and (b) presence of Fe3+; (c) the linear equation plot of F0-F/F0*100 against the Fe3+ concentration.

(a) The PL spectra of NGQDs/Fe3+ in presence of GSH (0–1000 μM); (b) trend of regain in fluorescence intensity with addition of GSH with inset showing the fluorescence of (i) NGQDs, (ii) NGQDs/Fe3+, (iii) NGQDs/Fe3+/GSH; (c) the linear equation plot of FR/F0*100 against the GSH concentration.
Fluorescence intensities change of the NGQDs in the presence of different interfering substances.
The molecular structure and absorption spectrum of modeled structure for synthesized probe.
The complex structure formed between ferric and modeled structure.

Images of HeLa cells (a) untreated (bright field); (b) probe treated HeLa cells; (c) Doxorubicin (std) treated HeLa cells; (d) HeLa cells bright field; (e) HeLa cells untreated-blue channel (f) HeLa cells blue channel (treated with probe); (g) HeLa cells bright field; (h) HeLa cells blue channel (treated with GSH); (i) HeLa cells green channel (merged).
Schematic representation of synthesis procedure of NGQDs.
A comparison between the reported methods and the proposed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *N, S co-doped carbon dots: NSCDs, Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots: NCDs; CA- Citric acid; OPDA- o-phenylenediamine; EA-ethanol amine; EDA- ethylene diamine.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HOMO and LUMO energies of molecules/ion obtained using the DFT calculations.
Detection of GSH in healthy human serum.

head.ce:title: Application of surface nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in the sensing of ferric ions and glutathione: Spectroscopic investigations and DFT calculations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur, Dist. Dhule, Maharashtra 425 405, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research Shirpur, Dist. Dhule, Maharashtra 425 405, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally as the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing a sensing platform that can quickly and accurately measure glutathione (GSH) is crucial for the early detection of various human diseases. GQDs have shown great potential in many technological and biological applications. This study focused on synthesizing nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) with stable blue fluorescence using a simple and easy hydrothermal method in one step. The bamboo fiber was used as the green source for this synthesis. The NGQDs had a tiny particle size of 4.7 nm and emitted light at 405 nm when excited. They displayed a remarkable quantum yield of 40.36 % and were effectively used as fluorescent probe to specifically detect Fe3+. The energy transfer mechanism led to the NGQDs' fluorescence being deactivated by Fe3+ ions (turn- “off”). However, with the addition of GSH to the system, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs was reactivated (turn- “on”). Thus, a fluorescence turn “off–on” system was developed for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ and GSH. Using density functional theory (DFT), it was theoretically calculated that the surface of the fabricated NGQDs possess lone pairs of electrons on oxygens and doped nitrogen causing a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process to occur. This PET process was suppressed previously owing to complex formation between oxygen atoms of modeled structure and ferric ions. The sensing platform displayed a sensitive response to Fe3+ in the 1–1000 μM range with LOD of 34 nM and GSH in the range of 1–50 μM, with a detection limit of 45 nM. Furthermore, the NGQDs exhibited high selectivity towards Fe3+ and GSH over other electrolytes and biomolecules. Additionally, the probe exhibited non-cytotoxicity and was practically applicable for the detection of GSH in HeLa cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (L-γ-glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine, GSH) is a significant non-protein biothiol composed of glutamate, cysteine, and glycine in mammalian cells. The GSH plays a vital role in various biological processes, for example, antioxidants, defence, regulating intracellular redox state, modulating cell growth, and assessing cellular health [1]. The GSH concentrations can vary depending on the type of cell or bodily fluid being measured. In normal cells, GSH concentrations typically range from 1 to 10 millimolar (mM). In contrast, GSH concentrations in plasma are typically lower, ranging from 1 to 6 µM (µM) [2,3]. From the total GSH in normal healthy cells and tissues approximately more than 90 % exists in the reduced form (reduced-GSH) whereas less than 10 % exists as (GSSG) which is an oxidized form of GSH [4]. Alteration in the levels of GSH can be an indication of conditions like cancer [5], myocardial infarction [6], Alzheimer’s disease [7], Parkinson’s disease [8], autism [2], tuberculosis [9], diabetes and HIV [10] where the decrease in GSH levels results in oxidative stress making the early detection of GSH important. Techniques mostly applied for the detection of GSH involve electrochemical detection techniques [11], surface enhanced Raman scattering [12,13], colorimetric analysis using nanocomposites and related agents [14,15], chromatographic techniques [16], etc. These strategies have proved to be effective detection however the major shortcoming namely cost, complicated instrumentation, low sensitivity and complexity of analysis makes them unproductive and restrict their use. Despite the challenges, fluorescence sensing has emerged as a highly promising technique for the detection of various biomolecules [17].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are novel forms of carbon nanomaterials that possess several unique advantages such as fluorophores [18]. The properties of GQDs could be drastically tuned by nitrogen-doping [19]. The various nitrogen doped Graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have electronic transport confined in all three spatial dimensions along with a non-zero bandgap, a reason for its luminescence on excitation [20]. The NGQDs demonstrate excellent interaction with a range of metal ions and this property has been widely exploited for the fabrication of biosensor systems for the detection of GSH [21,22] however the major negative aspect of the developed probe was the harmful nature of the metal ions utilized in the system can have deleterious impacts on human health when used for biosensing and bioimaging. As an effective alternative to these metal ions, ferric ion (Fe3+) can be utilized since it is comparatively harmless as well as it is present in biological systems [23]. Hence, this could serve in the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ in the biological systems making the probe of excellent importance as it can facilitate detection of iron deficiency in cases like anemia, liver injury, etc. Furthermore, the proposed research work relied on the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of NGQDs with stable blue fluorescence using bamboo fiber as a green source. This approach simplifies the production process and reduces environmental impact, making it a sustainable and eco-friendly method for NGQDs synthesis. Furthermore, the use of density functional theory (DFT) to theoretically calculate the surface properties of the fabricated NGQDs, such as the presence of lone pairs of electrons on oxygens and doped nitrogen, and their role in photo-induced electron transfer (PET) processes, adds a deeper understanding of the underlying mechanisms driving the sensing platform's performance. Demonstrating the practical applicability of the NGQDs probe for detecting GSH in HeLa cells is another notable aspect of this study. It highlights the potential of this sensing platform for biological and medical applications. The probe's non-cytotoxicity is essential for its potential use in biological and medical applications, indicating its safety for cellular studies.

In this work, the NGQDs were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal approach utilizing bamboo fiber and urea. The as-prepared NGQDs demonstrated excellent aqueous solubility and strong blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination. The fluorescence intensity of these NGQDs was selectively quenched after incorporation of Fe3+ ions via a reduced charge transfer process termed as fluorescence turn “off”. Addition of GSH into this NGQDs/Fe3+ system resulted in a considerable regain of fluorescence of NGQDs on account of the reaction between the thiol group (-SH) present on the surface of GSH and the Fe3+ ions where a redox reaction results in the conversion of Fe3+ into Fe2+ with simultaneous conversion of GSH into its oxidized form i.e. GSSG [24]. This regain is termed as “turn on” of fluorescence. Consequently, this whole fluorescence turn “off–on” system was employed for the detection of Fe3+ as well as GSH in aqueous media (pH 7) at 340 nm excitation using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Rettenmaier India Pvt. Ltd., located in Thane, India, generously provided the bamboo fiber used in this study. Chemicals including urea (CH4N2O), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4⋅5H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), mercury chloride (HgCl2), glycine (Gly, C2H5NO2), sodium chloride (NaCl), lysine (Lys, C6H14N2O2), cysteine (Cys, C3H7NO2S), uric acid (C5H4N4O3), glucose (C6H12O6), and quinine sulfate (C20H24N2O2) were obtained from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. in India. glucose, uric acid (UA), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), Alanine (Ala), Asparagine (Asp), Dopamine (DA), Methionine (Meth), Phenyl alanine (Phe), Leucine (Leu), Iso Leucine (Iso Leu), Serine (Ser), Threonine (Thr), Valine (Val), Proline (Pro), and Homocysteine (Hcys) were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. in India. Potassium chloride (KCl) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Merck, India. Glutathione (GSH, C10H17N3O6S) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Analytical grade chemicals were used for all experiments and were used as received. Ultrapure water was used to prepare all solutions.

The UV–Visible absorption spectra were acquired using a UV 1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). To determine the functional groups, present on the surface of NGQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using an IR Infinity DRS 8000 technique (Shimadzu, Japan) with KBr pellet. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were observed by Bruker 15,302 FESEM/ EDX with AC voltage of 15 kV at 1 μm resolution and the elemental analysis of the NGQDs was done by the same instrument with 20 kV accelerating voltage. The morphology and microstructure were observed using the Philips TEM CM 200 at AC of 200 kV. The size of particles in the aqueous samples of NGQDs was analyzed using the Nanoplus 3 Particulate System from Micromeritics (USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Jasco FP8200 Spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan) with a 5 nm slit width for both excitation and emission. A Bruker RFS 27 Stand Alone FT-Raman Spectrometer was used for recording Raman spectra of synthesized probes.

To synthesize NGQDs, a hydrothermal approach was employed using bamboo fiber powder as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. Firstly, 200 mg of bamboo fiber powder and 100 mg of urea were dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 5 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was then transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon lined hydrothermal reactor and carbonized at 200 °C for a period of 8 h [25,26]. Once the reaction was completed, the reactor was allowed to cool down and the dark brown product solution was collected and filtered. The obtained solution was then dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (12 kDa) and double distilled water as the dialysate. After dialysis, the NGQDs solution was lyophilized to obtain a dry powder which was then collected and stored at 4 °C for further use. The NGQDs synthesized by this method were found to possess unique optical properties and were investigated for their potential application as fluorescent probes for detecting biomolecules such as Fe3+ and GSH.

The quantum yield (QY) of NGQD was determined using a comparative method as described in reference [27]. To determine the QY, quinine sulfate was selected as a standard with a known QY value of 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4. Dilutions of the quinine sulfate standard were prepared using distilled water. Hereafter, absorbance was measured at 350 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, with optical density < 0.1 to minimize the reabsorption effect. The emission spectra of quinine sulfate standard and synthesized NGQD were recorded at 350 nm excitation on a spectrofluorometer, and a graph of integrated fluorescence intensity verses absorbance was plotted. The QY of NGQD was then determined using the following equation:

The QY of NGQDs was calculated using the following equation –
φ x
=
φ
st
Grad
x
Grad
st
η x
η
st
Where, φ is QY and the subscripts x and st represents sample (NGQD) and standard (quinine sulfate) respectively, Grad depicted the slope of fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance plot, η representing the refractive index of the distilled water.

NGQDs were utilized for detecting Fe3+ in distilled water (pH 7.0) at room temperature with high selectivity. To determine the sensitivity of NGQDs towards Fe3+, numerous concentrations of Fe3+ ions were incorporated to an aqueous solution of NGQDs (0.1 mg/mL, 1500 μL), resulting in final concentrations ranging from 1 to 1000 μM. The solutions were incubated for 10 min, and emission spectra were recorded at 340 nm excitation. Similarly, to detect other positive ions (at a concentration of 1000 μM), the same procedure was followed, and PL spectra were observed with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm by a fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing study using NGQDs/Fe3+ for GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of NGQDs/Fe3+ towards GSH, in 2 mL of NGQDs/Fe3+ solution (0.1 mg mL−1 NGQD, 100 μM Fe3+, pH 7.0) different concentrations of GSH solution were added making the final concentration of GSH was in the range of (0–1000 μM). After mixing the solution, it was incubated for 10 min, and the spectral measurements were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. To detect other biological molecules at a concentration of 1000 μM, a similar procedure was performed, and the PL spectra were observed with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm by a fluorescence spectrometer.

The optimization of the modeled structures was carried out through density functional theory (DFT) applying the Becke three-parameter hybrid density functional method (B3LYP) using a basis set 6-31G(d) for carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen atoms. Time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) with the CAM-B3LYP method was employed for HOMO-LUMO calculations [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of serum sample for GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a frozen serum sample was collected from a healthy individual selected at the hospital. To eliminate interfering proteins, 1 mL of the serum sample was combined with an equal volume of acetonitrile solution and allowed to coagulate for 5 min, as reported in [29]. Following coagulation, centrifugation was performed at 15,000 rpm for 10 min, yielding a semi-transparent supernatant layer enriched with biothiols. The next step involved diluting the serum sample tenfold in PBS for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopy of probe for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were initially cultured by seeding them into 96-well plates, with each well containing 10,000 cells suspended in 200 µL of culture medium. These plates were then placed in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C and left to incubate overnight. Following this incubation period, the old culture medium was carefully removed from all wells, and the cells were treated with a probe solution containing 110 μg/mL of NGQDs/Fe3+ in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS. The cells were allowed to interact with the probe for a duration of 24 h. Afterward, a series of washing steps using Buffered Phosphate Saline (BPS) were carried out to eliminate any remaining probe or culture medium. Subsequently, the HeLa cells were stained with a Hoechst 33,342 solution (5 μg/ml) in a dark environment for 10 mins. Finally, fluorescent live cell imaging was performed with an excitation wavelength of 361 nm and emission observed at 497 nm, utilizing the FITC (Fluorescein isothiocyanate) channel for visualization at 650 nm excitation and 525 nm emission wavelengths.

The synthesis of NGQDs using bamboo fiber and urea was depicted in Scheme 1 . The NGQDs synthesis was carried out using a facile one step hydrothermal approach in which bamboo fiber powder as source for carbon and urea as source of nitrogen were used in the ratio of 2:1 at 200 °C where cellulose and hemicellulose present in bamboo fiber gets interlinked during the synthesis process forming the smaller graphitic units which consequently grow to form the GQD structure with simultaneous doping of the nitrogen on the graphitic surface. Nitrogen doping enhanced the quantum yield of the GQDs and the functional groups possessing nitrogen and oxygen in NGQDs upgrade their capability of chelation with metal ions [30].

The UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were studied to explore the optical properties of NGQDs. The Fig. 1 a, display the UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs, where a prominent absorption peak at 268 nm which is attributed to π → π* transition of the aromatic C = C sp2 domains [31] and can be observed along with a shoulder peak at 300 nm which is caused by n → π* transition of the C = O bond as well as transition of the conjugate structure [30,32]. Under the Visible light, the aqueous solution of NGQDs appears slight pale-yellow color and under long UV (365 nm), it emits strong blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 1a).

Further, investigation of PL behavior of the synthesized NGQDs at different excitation wavelength from 270 nm to 400 nm was conducted as shown in Fig. 1b, which showed a rise in the emission intensity at 405 nm with the increase in the excitation wavelength from 270 nm to 340 nm. However, with further increase in the excitation from 350 nm to 400 nm there was steady decrease in the emission intensity with simultaneous shifting of the emission peak towards longer wavelength. These results showed that the NGQDs demonstrated an excitation independent emission up to 340 nm which later changed to excitation dependent behavior at longer wavelengths [33]. This behavior probably resulted from the internal nature of NGQD structure perhaps due to the energy released by the smaller graphitic domains.

The NGQDs had a quantum yield as high as 40.36 % with high fluorescence intensity over a range of pH from 5 to 8 with strongest fluorescence at neutral pH. However, in strong acidic and alkaline environments the fluorescence intensity reduced considerably as depicted in Fig. S1. Thus, the NGQDs were viable over a range of workable pH with good photostability and hence the pH 7.0 was chosen as the ideal sensing condition for all the experiments. In addition, the stability study of the synthesized probe for Inter-day, Intra Day (Table S1) and for three months was evaluated (Table S2) which proved that the probe is stable for up to 3 months.

An FTIR spectroscopy was accomplished to identify various functional groups and surface chemistry of NGQDs. As per Fig. 2 a, characteristic absorption peaks were monitored at 3390.97 cm−1 and 2927.08 cm−1 relating to broad O–H stretching and C–H stretching respectively [34]. The unstable double bond C = C stretching is observed at 1604.83 cm−1 along with 1388.79 cm−1 representing N–H bending. The strong and sharp peak at 1112.96 cm−1 was ascribed to C-O alkoxy peak (str) while peak at 618.21 cm−1 represents the aromatic C–H bending.

EDX analysis (Fig. 2b) confirmed the successful nitrogen doping on the NGQDs. The Raman analysis helps in confirming the successful formation of the graphitic structure in the GQDs. From Fig. 2c, G-band at 1300 cm−1 which relates to the graphitic domains in the structure and a D band at 1611 cm−1 representing the defects present in the graphitic structure[35] of the NGQDs can be observed.

The average particle size of the as prepared NGQDs was analyzed to be in the range of 2–20 nm (Fig. 3 a). The morphology of the fabricated NGQDs was characterized by SEM and TEM. As per Fig. 3b, the SEM images show the uniform and continuous sheet-like structure with appreciably rough surface and no visible surface distortion. Herein, Fig. 3c shows the TEM image of the synthesized NGQDS which were present in uniform cluster form with observable dot structure representing individual NGQDs particles which were spherical and well dispersed. The average particle size of the NGQDs was discovered as 5 nm that can be assumed from image analysis of all individual particles. The SAED pattern Fig. 3d depicts the sharp ring pattern suggesting the crystalline nature of the NGQDs.

The feasibility of using Fe3+ as fluorescence quencher for the NGQDs was explored in aqueous media. When Fe3+ in the concentration range from 0 to 1000 μM was added to the NGQDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1, pH 7.0), the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs at 405 nm gradually decreased termed as fluorescence turn “off”. The fluorescence emission study of the NGQDs in presence of Fe3+ at excitation of 340 nm is shown in Fig. 4 a where the gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity with increasing Fe3+ concentration is observed. The change in the fluorescence intensity from bright blue to no fluorescence was also observed under UV irradiation (the inset of Fig. 4b). The mechanism of the discerning fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on NGQDs is acknowledged by the weakened photo electron transfer (PET) process due to interaction between NGQDs and Fe3+ ions. The Fe3+ coordinated with the phenolic hydroxyl (–OH) groups which were present in abundance on the NGQDs surface, facilitating the bridging of the neighboring NGQDs leading to fluorescence turn “off” probably because of the aggregation of the NGQDs due to the Fe3+ ions which leads to a metal-quenching effect [36]. Similar to earlier reported research, the interaction of oxygen functionalities such as phenolic hydroxyl (–OH) available on the surfaces of synthesized NGQDs with Fe3+ ions forms non fluorescent metallic complexes resulting in PL quenching [37,38]. The –OH groups on the surface of the NGQDs act as Lewis bases, and Fe3+ ions function as Lewis’s acids in this context. When Fe3+ ions are introduced into the NGQD solution, they readily coordinate with the surface –OH groups to form coordination complexes. This interaction leads to the transfer of electrons from the NGQDs to the Fe3+ ions, effectively reducing the NGQDs and quenching their PL. As a result, the formation of these non-fluorescent complexes enhances the specificity of NGQDs towards Fe3+ ions, as evidenced by performing DFT calculations.

Furthermore, the functional groups encompassing nitrogen and oxygen on the surfaces of NGQDs improve the ability of chelation with Fe3+ ions [30]. The Fig. 4b presents the relation of fluorescence quenching value F0-F/F0*100 (where F0 and F were the PL intensity emission at 405 nm of NGQDs upon excitation of 340 nm in the absence and presence of quencher respectively) with the increasing Fe3+ concentration. The linear relation of fluorescence quenching with R2 = 0.9863 from (1–1000) μM Fe3+ concentration with calibration equation F0-F/F0*100 = 0.0652 [Fe3+] + 10.168 is displayed in Fig. 4c. The detection limit was calculated to be 34 nM (S is the slope and δ is the standard deviation) using equation 3σ/S rule [39]. Approximately 75.57 % quenching of the fluorescence intensity was found to be with 1000 μM Fe3+ concentration while 8.66 % quenching was recorded at concentration as low as 1 μM. From these results, it was observed that fluorescence quenching of NGQDs by the Fe3+ was sensitive and quenching of fluorescence of NGQDs over a wide range concentration of Fe3+ ions can be achieved.

To explore the interaction mechanisms between NGQDs and Fe3+, we used a Stern − Volmer plot. Typically, fluorescence quenching results from interaction ns between fluorescence and quenching molecules, either through dynamic or static quenching. The Stern -Volmer equation,
F
Fo
= 1 + k s v
[ Q ]
was employed, with F and F0 representing NGQDs' fluorescence intensities with and without Fe3+ at 340 nm excitation, [Q] denoting quencher concentration, and ksv as the quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer plot displayed a linear trend within the Fe3+ concentration range of 1–1000 μM. The calculated ksv was 1.31 × 104 M−1 (in μM units), indicating static quenching due to the formation of nonfluorescent complexes from the association of ground-state fluorescence molecules with quenching agents i.e., Fe3+. This perhaps aims to provide a more concise yet comprehensive explanation of the Stern-Volmer plot and its significance in uncovering the quenching mechanisms in our study.

To evaluate the performance of the as prepared NGQDs for detection of GSH, a study was conducted by adding varying concentration of GSH (1–1000 μM) into the NGQDs/Fe3+ solution (0.1 mgmL−1 NGQD, 100 μM Fe3+) in aqueous media. The addition of GSH to NGQDs/Fe3+ solution resulted in the recovery of fluorescence which was visible under UV lamp which is shown in inset of Fig. 5 b. Fig. 5a shows PL spectra of the regain of the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs upon addition of different concentrations of GSH to NGQDs/Fe3+ probe. The PL spectra demonstrated that the developed system successfully detected GSH, as evidenced by a consistent fluorescence regain observed upon the addition of GSH at various concentrations. The trend in the fluorescence recovery of the NGQDs with the gradual increasing GSH concentration is shown in Fig. 5b where the concentration of NGQDs and Fe3+ was kept constant [40]. The liner ranges from 1 to 50 μM of GSH against the fluorescence regain with linear equation FR/F0*100 = 0.1612[GSH] + 30.656 and correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9413 is depicted in Fig. 5c. The detection limit of 45 nM was identified using the 3σ/S rule. The probable reason for the observed fluorescence restoration could be the high affinity of GSH towards ferric ions, which may result in a redox reaction between GSH and Fe3+. This reaction can lead to the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ and the consequent conversion of GSH into its oxidized form, GSSG. This process may result in the regeneration of the fluorescent signal in the developed system. This reduction of Fe3+ detaches the ions from the NGQDs surface which restores the fluorescence intensity. The results from these studies confirmed that the synthesized NGQDs and the developed NGQDs/Fe3+ system was effective in sensitive detection of GSH. Table 1 illustrates a comparison of alternative methods with the recently reported “off-on” fluorescent probes utilizing carbon dots and metal ions.

To investigate the explicit ability of NGQDs towards Fe3+, the fluorescence emissions of NGQDs in presence of range of positive ions (1000 μM) were considered at excitation of 340 nm and the plot of fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 6 . The ions added to the NGQDs solution (N+, K+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Co2+ and Ag+) did not significantly change the fluorescence intensity. While Fe3+ triggered a substantial change in the fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, we tested the interaction of NGQDs directly with GSH and found that GSH does not cause any considerable quenching (Fig. S2). The decrease in the intensity (F/F0) by N+, K+ ions which are normally present in buffer systems and biological fluid systems was least and hence these ions were negligible interferents making the probe suitable for application in biological systems. The ions Cu2+, Co2+ and Ag+ quenched the fluorescence slightly while Hg2+ caused significant quenching almost 26.78 % which can be justified owing to the fact that there occurs electron or energy transfer between the negatively charged molecules (carboxyl and hydroxyl) of NGQDs and Hg2+ leading to quenching [1,49]. Although there was quenching in presence of Hg2+ still the amount of quenching obtained by Fe3+ (54.68 %) was maximum. To validate the restoration of fluorescent by GSH, different biological molecules (1000 μM) including glucose, uric acid (UA), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), Homocysteine (Hcys), leucine (Leu), Glycine (Gly), Alanine (Ala), Asparagine (Asp), Dopamine (DA), Methionine (Meth), Phenyl alanine (Phe), Leucine (Leu), Iso Leucine (Iso Leu), Serine (Ser), Threonine (Thr), Valine (Val), Proline (Pro) were added into the NGQD/Fe3+ and the Fig. 6 depicts the change in fluorescence of NGQDs/Fe3+ system (F/F0) as recorded at excitation of 340 nm in presence and absence of biological molecules. The uric acid, glucose, Gly, Ala, Asp, DA, Meth, Phe, Leu, Iso Leu, Ser, Thr, Val, Pro and thiol amino acids Hcys, and Cys did not exhibit a significant influence on fluorescence intensities [50]. Even though they possess the similar functional behavior the recovery of fluorescence by GSH was highest ∼ 92.76 % in comparison to other biomolecules tested thus establishing the applicability and reliability of the developed probe for the sensitive and selective detection of GSH even in presence of range of interfering biomolecules.

Theoretical calculations were utilized to investigate the role of Fe3+ in quenching of fluorescence intensity of NGQDs via complex formation. And similarly, the selectivity in regain of fluorescence intensity of probe after incorporating GSH has been explored. Herein, the optical properties of a series of aromatic based structures were investigated using DFT methods [51]. Among these, an aromatic based modeled structure was selected as depicted in Fig. 7 . This modeled structure (C9H13NO) presented the closest optical properties of maximum UV peak at 268 nm similar to fabricated probe.

Interestingly, Fe3+ ions form complex with oxygens of modeled structure via redox reaction, and this complexation hinders the availability of lone pair of electrons on the surfaces of oxygen causing PET process to stop (“turn -off”). This transfer of electrons is supported by calculations of LUMO energies of Fe3+ ions and modeled structure as depicted in Table 2 , which confirms that the difference of energies is higher to bring about the redox reaction forming complex structure as depicted in Fig. 8 . Subsequently, after addition of GSH to the complex, the thiol functional group of GSH forms a new complex with ferric ions competitively, causing detachment of modeled structure and regain of fluorescence intensity of probe via “turn- on” PET process. This interaction of GSH with ferric-modeled structure complex can be supported by the fact that HOMO energy of GSH (-0.23) is close to the LUMO energy of ferric-modeled structure complex (-0.22) that forms new bond between thiol (-SH) of GSH and ferric with subsequent separation of probe (modeled structure) [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GSH in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effectiveness of the proposed method, a nano probe (NGQD/Fe3+) was utilized to monitor the levels of reduced glutathione (GSH) in human serum. Initially, the concentration of GSH was estimated in a non-spiked serum sample. Subsequently, a standard addition method was employed to detect GSH concentrations [53], and the results were presented in Table 3 . The spiked serum samples consistently exhibited GSH concentrations with recoveries ranging from 98.25 to 101.33 percent, with a relative standard deviation (% RSD) of less than 3.5. These findings demonstrated the practical application of the NGQD/Fe3+ fluorophore sensor for accurate GSH detection in human serum samples in accordance with previous literature [54].

We studied the detection of GSH using (NGQDs/Fe3+) probe in HeLa cell by performing live imaging. Before conducting the GSH detection assay, we first assessed the probe's cytotoxicity using the MTT method. As depicted in Fig. 9 (a), (b), and (c), HeLa cells were exposed to various concentrations of the probe for 8 h. Remarkably, the cell survival rate remained consistently above 89 % even as the probe concentration increased. These results unequivocally demonstrated the probe's minimal cytotoxicity, establishing its suitability for fluorescence imaging within live cells. Fig. 9 (d), (e), and (f) depict the outcomes when cells were incubated with the probe for 30 min at 37 °C, following a 35 min pretreatment with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM), a thiol scavenger (used as a control). The absence of green fluorescence in these conditions confirms the quenched state and the absence of available GSH at the cellular site. Subsequently, HeLa cells treated with GSH (1000 μM) exhibited robust green fluorescence, as seen in Fig. 9 (g), (h), and (i). These results provide compelling evidence that the probe can indeed serve as an effective tool for detecting GSH within live cells.

In this study, a facile, one-step hydrothermal approach was used to prepare NGQDs by utilizing bamboo fiber and urea as the suitable carbon and nitrogen source respectively which were employed for the selective detection of GSH in presence of Fe3+ ions. The TEM and Raman analysis proved the successful synthesis of the NGQDs with a high quantum yield of 40.36 %. The synthesized NGQDs were found to be applicable for the turn “off–on” fluorescence sensing of the Fe3+ and GSH. The Fe3+ efficiently quenched the fluorescence of NGQDs over a linear range of 1–1000 μM with a high selectivity and upon the introduction of GSH into the NGQDs/Fe3+ the subsequent restoration of fluorescence with a LOD of 45 nM. This method offered a linear GSH detection range of 1–50 μM. In this study, the modeled structures underwent optimization using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the B3LYP method and a 6-31G(d) basis set. The calculations for Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) were performed using time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) with the CAM-B3LYP method. The results of these DFT calculations provide support for the formation of a complex that interrupts the PET process, followed by the subsequent addition of GSH, which leads to the recovery of fluorescence intensity in the probe. The NGQDs were found to be highly sensitive towards Fe3+ and GSH even in presence of various interferents and the developed probe can be used for the detection of Fe3+ and GSH with high reliability and selectivity. The proposed method suffers few restrictions such as the theoretical calculations using DFT may require specialized computational resources and expertise, which could be a limitation for some researchers. And the study does not delve into the mechanisms of cellular uptake and delivery in detail, which could be an area for further investigation in future studies.

In summary, this study distinguishes itself through its innovative use of bamboo fiber as a green source material, the one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, the development of a dual-analyte fluorescence “off-on” system, theoretical insights using DFT calculations, practical application in HeLa cells, and an eco-friendly approach. These unique aspects collectively contribute to the advancement of NGQDs based sensing platforms for various applications, including disease detection and environmental monitoring.

Zamir G. Khan: Conceptualization, Software. Tanisha N. Agrawal: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sanjaykumar B. Bari: Supervision. Sopan N. Nangare: Writing – review & editing. Pravin O. Patil: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are highly obliged to the H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, (India), for providing necessary facilities. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by The Kavayitri Bahinabai Chaudhari North Maharashtra University, Jalgaon under Vice Chancellor Research Motivation Scheme (VCRMS), (KBCNMU/11A/VCRMS/Budget-2021-22/Sci. & Tech./137/2022.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123608.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
